items.q: What is the value of x^0 (for x not equal to zero)?
items.correct: 1 — any non-zero number to the power of zero equals 1.
items.misconception: x^0 equals 0 because raising something to the zero gives nothing.
items.random_wrong: x^0 equals x because the zero has no effect.
items.why: Students reason 'zero of anything is zero', applying additive intuition to exponents — they don't see x^0 as the result of x^1 / x^1 = 1.

items.q: On a speed-time graph, what does the area under the line represent?
items.why: Students don't link 'area = speed x time = distance' and instead try to read distance off the y-axis.

items.q: If 5 pens cost £3.50, how much do 7 pens cost?
items.correct: £4.90 — find the unit cost (£0.70 per pen), then multiply by 7.
items.misconception: £5.50, by adding £2 to £3.50 because there are 2 more pens.
items.why: Students apply additive thinking (more pens means add more pounds) instead of multiplicative reasoning via the unit rate.

items.q: A force of 10 N acts perpendicular to a rod, 0.5 m from the pivot. Moment is:
items.why: Correct answer: 5 Nm. “20 Nm” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: For a body in equilibrium, the sum of moments about any point is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: zero. “positive” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Find the moment of a 20 N force acting perpendicular to a lever, 2 m from the pivot.
items.why: Exact answer: 40 Nm. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
